meta.title: Is Icon Veneration a Big Deal? What Most People Miss
meta.author: Gavin Ortlund

questions.question: How does Ortlund characterize Protestant concern about icon veneration?
options: Protestants invented the issue to divide ecumenical dialogue
Protestants are mainly responding to how Nicaea II and its reception make the issue binding for Catholicism and Eastern Orthodoxy
Protestants reject all religious art as idolatrous by definition
Protestants treat icon style as more important than the Trinity
questions.answer: Protestants are mainly responding to how Nicaea II and its reception make the issue binding for Catholicism and Eastern Orthodoxy

questions.question: What three-part structure does the video use to organize the argument?
options: Art history, economics, and politics
Why icon veneration matters, what it is at Nicaea II, and when it began
Scripture, tradition, and reason only
East, West, and Oriental Orthodox jurisdictions
questions.answer: Why icon veneration matters, what it is at Nicaea II, and when it began

questions.question: According to Ortlund (citing Richard Price), what can "iconoclasm" mean without entailing destruction of images?
options: Accepting only black-and-white icons
Opposing the veneration of sacred images
Rejecting the doctrine of the Trinity
Removing all crosses from churches
questions.answer: Opposing the veneration of sacred images

questions.question: Which council does Ortlund identify as the seventh ecumenical council and the definitive late-eighth-century iconophile settlement?
options: The Council of Chalcedon (451)
The First Council of Nicaea (325)
The Second Council of Nicaea (787)
The Council of Trent
questions.answer: The Second Council of Nicaea (787)

questions.question: Which pairing does Ortlund use as examples of major iconoclast-affiliated councils?
options: Trent and Vatican I
Ephesus and Constantinople II
The Council of Hieria and the Council of Frankfurt
Nicea I and Constantinople I
questions.answer: The Council of Hieria and the Council of Frankfurt

questions.question: What does Ortlund say Nicaea II makes icon veneration for Catholic and Orthodox self-understanding?
options: A private devotional option bishops may ignore
A potential falsification test for claims about infallible, irreformable conciliar teaching
A matter of canon law only in monasteries
Equivalent to musical style in worship
questions.answer: A potential falsification test for claims about infallible, irreformable conciliar teaching

questions.question: In the post-conciliar letter to the emperor and Irene, how do the bishops define anathema?
options: A temporary suspension from receiving the Eucharist
Separation from God
A recommendation to study patristics further
Exclusion only from political office
questions.answer: Separation from God

questions.question: Which figure does Ortlund cite as patriarch of Constantinople using severe imagery (e.g., outer darkness) for anathema?
options: John Chrysostom
Athanasius of Alexandria
Tarasius
Photius
questions.answer: Tarasius

questions.question: Which biblical author does Ortlund mention as a parallel for how "anathema" can function?
options: Paul (e.g., Galatians)
James
The author of Hebrews only
John in Revelation exclusively
questions.answer: Paul (e.g., Galatians)

questions.question: What is Ortlund's first response to the idea that conciliar anathemas were narrowly scoped so they barely affect the main argument?
options: Anathemas were added centuries later by forgers
Even if scope were limited, Nicaea II would still be ecumenically decisive on the doctrine and practice
Nicaea II was never received in the East
Only laypeople were anathematized, never clergy
questions.answer: Even if scope were limited, Nicaea II would still be ecumenically decisive on the doctrine and practice

questions.question: What second point does Ortlund make about anathemas targeting people "inside" the church?
options: Only bishops could be anathematized
Given "no salvation outside the church" theology, being cast out still places one outside salvation
Insiders were immune from all penalties
The phrase only meant removal from a local parish roster
questions.answer: Given "no salvation outside the church" theology, being cast out still places one outside salvation

questions.question: What does Ortlund emphasize about the wording of the anathemas themselves?
options: They only condemn destroying art supplies
They are largely unqualified—targeting beliefs, behaviors, and associations—so limiters need period evidence
They explicitly exempt all Protestants
They apply only to emperors
questions.answer: They are largely unqualified—targeting beliefs, behaviors, and associations—so limiters need period evidence

questions.question: According to Ortlund (via Richard Price on the seventh session), what concrete acts are tied to icon veneration terminology?
options: Only silent meditation in a side chapel
Kisses and deep bow or prostration (plus related practices like candles/incense in honor of images)
Fasting on Wednesdays only
Singing metrical psalms without images present
questions.answer: Kisses and deep bow or prostration (plus related practices like candles/incense in honor of images)

questions.question: What recurring theological formula does Ortlund associate with Nicaea II's understanding of images?
options: Images replace the need for Scripture
What is given to the image passes through to the prototype
Images are mere decorations with no spiritual role
Only the bishop may see icons
questions.answer: What is given to the image passes through to the prototype

questions.question: Why does Ortlund reject analogies like putting one's hand on the heart during the Pledge of Allegiance?
options: He thinks all civic gestures are sinful
Those analogies do not match bowing, kissing, candles, and prototype theology as at Nicaea II
He denies that Americans have hearts
He claims icons are identical to national flags
questions.answer: Those analogies do not match bowing, kissing, candles, and prototype theology as at Nicaea II

questions.question: What does Ortlund say about the Council of Frankfurt and religious art?
options: It banned all painting
Its bishops could be very pro-art yet still reject Nicaea II
It mandated iconostases in every village
It was an iconophile council later reversed by Trent
questions.answer: Its bishops could be very pro-art yet still reject Nicaea II

questions.question: How does Ortlund describe Nicaea II's stance on doctrinal or liturgical "development" regarding icons?
options: The council explicitly theorized gradual development over centuries
The council treats theology and cultic practice as one apostolic piece, not a late add-on
The council admits icons began in the tenth century
The council leaves practice entirely to individual conscience
questions.answer: The council treats theology and cultic practice as one apostolic piece, not a late add-on

questions.question: What does Ortlund report Richard Price says impartial historians today would judge about the iconoclast historical claim?
options: It is obviously false
That reverential veneration of images did not go back to the apostles or the Golden Age fathers would be judged simply correct
Only Orthodox historians agree with iconophiles
There is no scholarly discussion of dating
questions.answer: That reverential veneration of images did not go back to the apostles or the Golden Age fathers would be judged simply correct

questions.question: What time window does Ortlund (following Price) give for scholarly debate about when cultic veneration of images emerges?
options: First century vs second century
Late seventh century vs slightly earlier seventh century
Only the sixteenth century
Exclusively the fourth century
questions.answer: Late seventh century vs slightly earlier seventh century

questions.question: What "Golden Age" patristic period does Price discuss regarding lack of explicit support for image veneration?
options: From Nicaea I (325) to Chalcedon (451)
From 787 to 1054 only
From the Reformation to today
From the apostolic fathers to Ignatius only
questions.answer: From Nicaea I (325) to Chalcedon (451)

questions.question: How does Ortlund characterize early Christian testimony about cultic use of images (figural mediation, bowing, etc.)?
options: Universally enthusiastic from day one
Broad agreement that such practice is pagan, not Christian—a hallmark distinction
Entirely silent
Limited to Gnostic sects only
questions.answer: Broad agreement that such practice is pagan, not Christian—a hallmark distinction

questions.question: Which early Christian writer does Ortlund quote as saying there is no religion wherever there is an image?
options: Augustine
Tertullian
Lactantius
Cyprian
questions.answer: Lactantius

questions.question: Which "father of church history" does Ortlund mention as shocked by a request for an icon?
options: Sozomen
Eusebius
Bede
Socrates Scholasticus
questions.answer: Eusebius

questions.question: How does Ortlund contrast challenges to biblical inerrancy with challenges to Nicaea II?
options: He denies any difference
Many inerrancy issues concern narrative details; Nicaea II is didactic conciliar teaching—if wrong in thrust, it is straightforwardly wrong
He argues Nicaea II is purely narrative
He claims inerrancy and Nicaea II stand or fall on the same verse
questions.answer: Many inerrancy issues concern narrative details; Nicaea II is didactic conciliar teaching—if wrong in thrust, it is straightforwardly wrong

questions.question: At the end, how does Ortlund rank Nicaea II compared to Frankfurt or Hieria without Roman/Orthodox reception theories?
options: Nicaea II alone is verbally inspired
Nicaea II is no more infallible than Frankfurt or Hieria; Scripture is God's speech in a way the council is not
Frankfurt alone is infallible
All three councils are equally inspired as Scripture
questions.answer: Nicaea II is no more infallible than Frankfurt or Hieria; Scripture is God's speech in a way the council is not
